label: 214_subsidies_fuel subsidies_subsidy reform_energy subsidies
description: Governments worldwide spend over $500 billion on fossil fuel subsidies, contributing to inefficiency and negative externalities. Efforts at reform have been limited, but the G20 committed to phasing them out in the medium term. The COVID-19 pandemic further highlighted the need for restructuring these systems.

earliest_articles.title: The Geopolitics of Electricity
earliest_articles.excerpt: A blackout in Italy won’t raise electricity prices in New York City. Unlike oil, electricity is not a globally traded commodity. Were the Strait of Hormuz to be shut down, consumers filling up at the pump in the U.S. would immediately feel the pinch. There is no such analogous situation for electricity. Still,…

newest_articles.title: Managing global economic headwinds: Lessons from Nigeria’s policy responses
newest_articles.excerpt: Factors external to Nigeria have long been important influences on policymaking and economic outcomes. Export revenues, diaspora transfers, foreign direct, and portfolio investments provide the principal channels through which global impulses are transmitted to Nigeria’s economy. central_articles.title: Reforming global fossil fuel subsidies: How the United States can restart international cooperation
central_articles.excerpt: Globally, governments spend more than $500 billion on subsidies for fossil fuels that contribute to inefficiency, inequity, and negative externalities. Despite this obvious problem, efforts at reforming fossil fuel subsidies across the world have been piecemeal. If countries are to achieve the decarbonization goals of…

central_articles.title: Counting thy numbers: Defining and measuring fossil fuel subsidies
central_articles.excerpt: Fossil fuel subsidies have attracted renewed attention following the Pittsburgh Summit of September 2009, where leaders of the G20 committed to “rationalise and phase out over the medium term inefficient fossil fuel subsidies that encourage wasteful consumption” (G20 2009).
